video_title: Why Wall Street's Biggest Traders Are Abandoning Crypto for Prediction Markets | Alex Momot
gist: Alex Momot, co-founder of market maker Peanut Trade, argues that collapsing crypto market-making margins are pushing top trading firms (Jane Street, Wintermute, Susquehanna, Jump) into prediction markets, where thin order books, high margins, and low barriers to entry create outsized opportunity. He sees liquidity provision, AI-driven cross-venue market consolidation, and AI-agent hedging as the next frontier, with Kalshi and Polymarket as dominant but vulnerable incumbents.

themes.theme: Capital rotation from crypto into prediction markets

insights.claim: Crypto generates only about 3% of the revenue of top market makers like Jane Street, whose annual revenue was roughly $40 billion, making expansion into prediction markets a profit-driven necessity rather than a strategic choice.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Signals that the largest TradFi quant shops will keep entering prediction markets to offset shrinking crypto margins; watch for Jane Street, Susquehanna, and Jump building prediction-market desks.
insights.quote: We know that only 3% of their revenue came from crypto.

insights.claim: Web3 market-making revenue is declining heavily, with daily MEV profits for bots collapsing to around $20K per day from far higher levels several years ago.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Confirms the structural erosion of on-chain trading edge; firms reliant on MEV/Web3 market making face margin compression and will seek new venues.
insights.quote: maybe several months ago it was 20K per day in profits for MEV bots. It means that it's significant decline

insights.claim: Wintermute has announced it will begin market making for prediction markets, having stayed in Web3-only markets until roughly 6-12 months ago, and more major players will follow quickly.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Front-runs a wave of institutional entrants into prediction-market liquidity; expect tightening spreads and consolidation in the next 12 months.
insights.quote: I can say that Wintermute delay their expense, I would say... But I think that we will have new players quite soon.

insights.claim: Prediction markets offer larger profit margins on smaller volume than crypto or TradFi, and lower competition than TradFi, letting small firms and individuals compete directly with giants like Jane Street, Susquehanna and Jump.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: The current window resembles the 2020 DeFi summer; small, fast capital can extract outsized returns before the giants saturate the space.
insights.quote: with a smaller volume it could be a larger profits. So margin much higher.

insights.claim: Prediction-market order books are so thin that a misclicked $1,000 market order can move the odds by 1.5%, a size that would be trivially absorbed even on a tier-three crypto exchange.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Quantifies how immature prediction-market liquidity is; slippage is the core unsolved problem and the main edge for incoming market makers.
insights.quote: just $1,000 could change the odds for 1.5%. So very thin order book it means that it's really hard for the regular user to trade.

insights.claim: On thin prediction markets it is often impossible not just to enter but to exit a position, so any new prediction-market platform should launch with a market maker already integrated to avoid a chicken-and-egg liquidity failure.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: A diligence checklist for new prediction-market entrants: no pre-committed liquidity provider at launch is a red flag; many Peanut clients delay launch until capital is deployed.
insights.quote: you should start only with the market maker because otherwise, it will be impossible not only to buy, but quite often it's impossible to exit from your position.

insights.claim: Peanut Trade sources liquidity from Kalshi and Polymarket and redeploys it into smaller prediction-market platforms by acting as maker on the small venue and taker on the large venue.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Explains the cross-venue arbitrage/liquidity-relay mechanism powering smaller markets; small-venue pricing is tethered to Kalshi/Polymarket for homogeneous markets.

themes.theme: Prediction markets as a superior data/price oracle

insights.claim: According to Peanut's internal research, Polymarket and Kalshi outperform expensive incumbent sports-book and event-data providers, making prediction markets a better real-time source of world-event data than legacy vendors.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Threatens incumbent data vendors and validates prediction markets as a tradable information feed; data-licensing could become a revenue line for these platforms.
insights.quote: prediction markets currently much better source for the actual data about something that's happening in the world than some kind of old players that became obsolete

insights.claim: Bitcoin price origination still happens on Coinbase, Binance and DEXes rather than on Polymarket or Kalshi, so accurate prediction-market pricing for crypto-linked markets must reference those external venues.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Defines a second layer of prediction-market making where fair value is imported from primary venues; edge comes from superior cross-venue pricing models.
insights.quote: if you will think about the price origination of Bitcoin, it's definitely not Polymarket and Kalshi for now.

insights.claim: Kalshi is now more often the origination point of alpha and the higher-volume, more institutional venue, with Polymarket more crypto-friendly and frequently only second in volume.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: For traders seeking the true price signal, Kalshi increasingly leads; Polymarket's crypto-native positioning may not translate to volume leadership.
insights.quote: the origination of alpha, we can say, so the original price. Now, more on Kalshi, I would say.

insights.claim: Insider trading is more visible on prediction markets than in Web3 or TradFi not because it is more prevalent but because crypto transparency and thin order books make privileged-information bets easy to detect and investigate.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Reframes insider-trading headlines as a transparency feature; the detectability that fuels bad press is also the platforms' compliance advantage.
insights.quote: if you know that some guy that flying together with American SWAT to Venezuela will make a bet and then FBI will do a research... we will know about that.

insights.claim: Insider-trading on prediction markets will be solved within several years through internal policies and account bans rather than threatening the sector's growth.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Suggests regulatory/compliance risk is manageable and not an existential threat to platform valuations; watch for codified resolution and trading rules.

insights.claim: Subjective oracle resolution, including strange UMA voting outcomes on Polymarket, is a major unsolved risk for niche markets where similarly-named markets carry different rules.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Resolution ambiguity is a concrete loss vector for traders; check rules before sizing, and treat oracle design as a key differentiator between platforms.
insights.quote: UMA voting on Polymarket sometimes quite strange for many users. And overall oracle problem and niche markets, it's something very subjective.

themes.theme: AI as the consolidation and hedging layer

insights.claim: Peanut is building an aggregated liquidity core plus an AI tool that compares markets across venues, merges liquidity for homogeneous markets, and updates positions according to each market's actual resolution rules.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Cross-venue liquidity aggregation with AI rule-parsing is the product roadmap to watch; it could become prediction markets' equivalent of a DEX aggregator.

insights.claim: Rather than thousands of fragmented markets, prediction markets will consolidate into a smaller number of indexes that bundle hot topics, such as a single Middle East escalation index or a Trump economy index.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Index-style products could be the dominant consumer interface; builders creating thematic indexes may capture flow currently fragmented across many markets.
insights.quote: I think that it will be indexes that will combine very hot topics in just one index... some kind of escalation in Middle East or Trump economy thoughts.

insights.claim: The endgame is personal AI agents that automatically hedge a user's real-world exposures via prediction markets, for example hedging Middle East escalation risk for a Dubai resident without the user managing rules, slippage or aggregation.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Positions prediction markets as a mainstream hedging/insurance primitive driven by AI-agent demand; a large potential B2B and retail flow source if realized.
insights.quote: if you live in Dubai, for example, you have a exposure to risk of escalation in the Middle East... your AI agent could do it for you.

insights.claim: The roughly $20 billion valuations attached to both Polymarket and Kalshi are hard to justify on volume growth alone given unresolved legal, niche-adoption and category-breadth questions.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: A skeptical read on the headline valuations; leaves room for mid-size differentiated competitors and questions late-stage entry prices.
insights.quote: 20 billion valuation for each of them is not something that could be justified because of just increasing numbers of volume.

insights.claim: Hyperliquid's approach to prediction markets, launching zero-commission user-created markets gated by staking the HYPE token, is smarter and more differentiated than Polymarket and Kalshi simply adding futures trading.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Hyperliquid targets larger-size traders and prop funds via permissionless market creation; its model is a credible challenger to incumbents' me-too expansions.
insights.quote: approach of Hyperliquid is much better than approach of Polymarket and Kalshi in opening some kind of futures trading.

insights.claim: Average trade size on Hyperliquid is much larger than on Polymarket and Kalshi, implying Hyperliquid serves a distinct higher-size trader segment rather than directly cannibalizing retail-oriented incumbents.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Segmentation by trade size suggests these venues may coexist; positioning matters more than head-to-head feature parity.
insights.quote: My bet is that the size of regular trade on Hyperliquid much bigger.

insights.claim: Peanut uses a retainer (algorithm-rental) pricing model for small prediction-market clients who lack volume for rebate programs, deliberately serving the segment that Jane Street and Susquehanna will not touch because integration costs are not worth it.
insights.speaker: Alex Momot
insights.so_what: Defines the niche-market gap below the giants; small platforms get day-one liquidity via retainers, and Peanut also monetizes launch consulting.
insights.quote: we introduced retainer model until smaller players will have their own rebate program.
